coredata.dc:title: A dual-mode probe for colorimetric and fluorometric detection of cysteine based on phosphorus/nitrogen co-doped CQDs and gold nanorods
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yanying
Liang, Ruoxuan
Liu, Wei
Zhao, Qingbiao
Zhu, Xiaoyan
Yang, Li
Zou, Ping
Wang, Xianxiang
Ding, Fang
Rao, Hanbing
coredata.dc:description: In this study a novel method was developed for the fluorescent and colorimetric determination of cysteine (Cys). The fluorescent determination was designed based on surface plasmon-enhanced energy transfer (SPEET), and phosphorus/nitrogen co-doped CQDs (PNCQDs) were employed as the donor, of which the fluorescence was quenched by gold nanorods (AuNRs) (acceptor). For the colorimetric assay, under the catalytic effect of Fe3+, AuNRs were effectively etched by OH· that was generated by the Fenton reaction. The etch of the AuNRs lead to the decrease in the absorbance intensity, the blue shift of longitudinal surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) absorption peak of AuNRs as well as the fluorescence of PNCQDs recovery. However, Cys can suppress this etching reaction by coordination effect between hydrosulfide group (SH) in Cys and Fe3+, leading to red shift of the LSPR absorption peak. Similarly, the fluorescence will change with the absorbance. The peak shift and the fluorescence change can be applied to monitor of Cys. Under the optimal operation conditions, highly sensitive determination of Cys was achieved, with the fluorescent detection limit of 1.2 nM and the colorimetric detection limit of 0.9 nM. The linearity of the system towards Cys was in the range of 0.005–25 μM. The highly sensitive determination of Cys in urine samples demonstrates that this method possesses high potential for on-site and visual detection of urine Cys.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cysteine
Gold nanorods
Inhibit reaction
Non-aggregation sensor
Dual-signal senor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of phosphorus/nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (PNCQDs)
Calculation of the fluorescence quantum yield
Characterizations of PNCQDs and gold nanorods
The mechanism of the sensing system
Effect of the concentration and volume of reagents
Effect of reaction temperature and time
Selectivity and interference of the sensor
Determination of Cys in human urine

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Two-Way Support Programs of Sichuan Agricultural University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by a grant from the Two-Way Support Programs of Sichuan Agricultural University (Project No. 03570113 ), the Education Department of Sichuan Provincial , PR China (Grant No. 16ZA0039 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the fluorometric and colorimetric assay for Cys detection based on AuNRs and PNCQDs.
HRTEM images of (a) PNCQDs, (b) AuNRs, the inset was SAED pattern of AuNRs (c) PNCQDs/AuNRs, (d) PNCQDs/AuNRs-HCl-Fe3+-H2O2, and (e) PNCQDs/AuNRs -HCl-Fe3+-H2O2-Cys.

(a) The absorbance spectra of AuNRs, PNCQDs/AuNRs, PNCQDs/AuNRs-HCl-Fe3+-H2O2, and PNCQDs/AuNRs-HCl-Fe3+-H2O2-Cys. (b) The fluorescence spectra of PNCQDs, PNCQDs/AuNRs, PNCQDs/AuNRs-HCl-Fe3+-H2O2, and PNCQDs/AuNRs -HCl-Fe3+-H2O2-Cys. Concentrations are as the following: 25.0 μM Cys, 30.0 mM HCl, 16.0 μM Fe3+, 80.0 μM H2O2.

(a) The fluorescence emission spectra of PNCQDs/AuNRs in the presence of different concentrations of Cys. (b) The relationship between (F0 − F)/F0 and the concentrations of Cys (n = 3). (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of the proposed assay in the presence of different amounts of Cys. (d) Relationship between the peak shift and the concentrations of Cys (n = 3).
(a) The peak shift and (b) the (F0 − F)/F0 in the presence of exogenous ions and compounds without or with 5.0 μM Cys.

head.ce:title: A dual-mode probe for colorimetric and fluorometric detection of cysteine based on phosphorus/nitrogen co-doped CQDs and gold nanorods

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University, Xin Kang Road, Yucheng District, Ya’an 625014, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanshan District Key Lab for Biopolymers and Safety Evaluation, Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Polymer Science and Technology, Guangdong Research Center for Interfacial Engineering of Functional Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanshan District Key Lab for Biopolymers and Safety Evaluation
Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Polymer Science and Technology
Guangdong Research Center for Interfacial Engineering of Functional Materials
College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Polar Materials and Devices, Ministry of Education, Department of Electronic Engineering, East China Normal University, Shanghai, 200241, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Polar Materials and Devices

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to the work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fluorescent of phosphorus/nitrogen co-doped CQDs (PNCQDs) was quenched by gold nanorods (AuNRs). under the catalytic of Fe3+, AuNRs were effectively etched by OH·, with the increase of etching depth, the absorbance of AuNRs gradual decrease as well as the fluorescence of PNCQDs recovery. However, Cys can forcefully suppress this etching reaction by means of coordination effect between hydrosulfide group (-SH) in Cys and Fe3+, thus, a dual-mode probe was established for colorimetric and fluorometric detection of cysteine.

In this study a novel method was developed for the fluorescent and colorimetric determination of cysteine (Cys). The fluorescent determination was designed based on surface plasmon-enhanced energy transfer (SPEET), and phosphorus/nitrogen co-doped CQDs (PNCQDs) were employed as the donor, of which the fluorescence was quenched by gold nanorods (AuNRs) (acceptor). For the colorimetric assay, under the catalytic effect of Fe3+, AuNRs were effectively etched by OH· that was generated by the Fenton reaction. The etch of the AuNRs lead to the decrease in the absorbance intensity, the blue shift of longitudinal surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) absorption peak of AuNRs as well as the fluorescence of PNCQDs recovery. However, Cys can suppress this etching reaction by coordination effect between hydrosulfide group (SH) in Cys and Fe3+, leading to red shift of the LSPR absorption peak. Similarly, the fluorescence will change with the absorbance. The peak shift and the fluorescence change can be applied to monitor of Cys. Under the optimal operation conditions, highly sensitive determination of Cys was achieved, with the fluorescent detection limit of 1.2 nM and the colorimetric detection limit of 0.9 nM. The linearity of the system towards Cys was in the range of 0.005–25 μM. The highly sensitive determination of Cys in urine samples demonstrates that this method possesses high potential for on-site and visual detection of urine Cys.

ce:section.ce:para: Cysteine (Cys) is an important amino acid in human body with essential physiological function. It is the only amino acid containing the reduced sulfhydryl group (-SH), among the 20 amino acids. Cys plays an important role in antioxidant, biological detoxification and physiological metabolism. It has potential neurotoxicity [1] for various morbid physiological environment [2]. Cys is a disease related physiological regulator [3] that has been applied to the medicine, food additives and cosmetics. Accurate determination of Cys can provide important insight in diagnosis of certain diseases [4]. Therefore, accurate detection of cysteine is of significant importance.

In previous literatures, several methods have been reported for determination of Cys, including electrochemical analysis [5], fluorescence analysis [6,7], mimic enzyme [8], electrochemiluminescence analysis [9], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10] and colorimetry [11–14], etc. However, these methods have certain disadvantages limiting their application. For instance, electrochemical method is time-consuming and instruments of HPLC are expensive. Thus, a selective, sensitive, inexpensive method for Cys detection is highly necessary.

With the excellent electrochemical [15,16] and optical properties, Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) have been applied in different fields. Due to the strong localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effect of AuNPs in the visible range, it can be adapted to diversified colorimetric assays [17]. In general, these colorimetric sensors have red shift based on LSPR, which is caused by the aggregation of nanoparticles [18–20]. In contrast to spherical AuNPs, gold nanorods (AuNRs) possess more predominant performance in optics and electrics, in the longitudinal plasmon band, AuNRs shows a strong optical extinction with the extinction coefficient of ∼6 × 109 M−1 cm−1, greater than that of gold nanoparticles (∼2.7 × 108 M−1 cm−1) [21]. Moreover, they are convenient to synthesize, and the change of colors accompanies the transformation of size dimension and surrounding medium [22]. Therefore, AuNRs have broad application in non-aggregation colorimetric sensors based on shape dependent LSPR property.

Fluorescence analysis has been extensively used in various fields due to its high sensitivity and simplicity [23,24]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new type of fluorescent materials, has been widely studied in recent years [24]. They exhibited great potential in many applications in various fields like optics [25], chemistry and biology. Owing to the excellent optical property such as high fluorescence quantum yields, superior photochemical stability, narrow and tunable fluorescent emission peak, CQDs have been widely used in fluorescence sensing analysis [7,26].

In determination of Cys, many factors may interfere with the readout of the single signal. Compared with these early methods, multiple signal detection provides multiple output modes at the same time, so that more reliable results can be obtained [27,28].

In this work, a dual-mode probe with both colorimetric and fluorometric readouts were developed for sensitive detection of Cys. In the presence of AuNRs, the fluorescence intensity of phosphorus/nitrogen co-doped CQDs (PNCQDs) quenched significantly. OH· can induce the etching of AuNRs, resulting in the fluorescence recovery of PNCQDs. When Cys was added, the etching progress would be limited, leading to the quench of the PNCQDs fluorescence. Based on that, the colorimetric and the fluorescent signal are effectively applied in the accurate examination of Cys. The proposed probe not only possesses high sensitivity and simplicity for fluorescence analysis, but also effectively eliminate the interference of background, making the result more reliable. Moreover, it is convenient to observed the color change of the system with bare eyes, allowing for feasible Cys identification.

Gold(III) chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O, ≥ 99.0%), glucose (C6H12O6, ≥ 99.0%), Cysteine (Cys, ≥ 98.5%), and 8 other common protein, phenylalanine (Phe, ≥ 99.0%), arginine (Arg, ≥ 98%), glutamic acid (Glu, ≥ 98.0%), glycine (Gly, ≥ 99.0%), histidine (His, ≥ 99.0%), lysine (Lys, ≥ 99.5%), proline (Pro, ≥ 99.0%), serine (Ser, ≥ 98.5%), glutathione (GSH, ≥ 99.0%), uric acid (UA, ≥ 99.0%), urea(≥ 99.0%), and silver nitrate (AgNO3, ≥ 99.0%) were purchased from Macklin (Shanghai, China). KCl (≥ 99.5%), NaCl (≥ 99.5%), FeCl3·6H2O (≥ 99.0%), HCl (36.0%–38.0%), NaBH4 (≥ 99.0%), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, ≥ 30%), 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA, ≥ 99.0%), concentrated phosphoric acid (H3PO4, ≥ 85.0%), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB, ≥ 99.0%) and ascorbic acid (AA, ≥ 99.7%) were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemistry Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China), ultrapure water used was generated through a Millipore system (18.23 MΩ cm), and used throughout the experiments.

Fluorescence spectra were collected with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer LS55), under the following conditions: excitation wavelength was 403 nm, emission range was 427–650 nm, excitation and emission monochromator slit widths were 10 nm and 20 nm, respectively. UV–vis absorption spectra from 400 to 800 nm were acquired with a UV–vis spectrophotometer (AnalytikJena Specord200Plus). Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) images, High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) measurements and selected area electron diffraction (SAED pattern) were performed with a field emission transmission electron microscope (JEOL-2100 F, Japan, accelerating voltage 300 kV). The hydrodynamic size and Zeta potential of the particles were characterized with a Malvern Zetasizer instrument (Malvern, Zetasizer Nano, UK).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of phosphorus/nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (PNCQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: PNCQDs were synthesized following Gong’s method [29]. (detailed in Supplementary information).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of the fluorescence quantum yield
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence quantum yield was measured following Han's method [30], all the experimental details are detailed in the Supplementary Information.
Gold nanorods were synthesized following the reported seed mediated method of El-Sayed with some modifications [31]. (detailed in Supplementary information).
The experimental procedures are detailed in the Supplementary Information, including detection procedure and sample preparation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of PNCQDs and gold nanorods
ce:section.ce:para: The PNCQDs were prepared by acid-base reaction heat method. Because of the foaming of the boiling glucose from the spontaneous heat (130 ℃), the volume of the glutinous slurry mixture was significantly extended. Within 30 s, the color of the mixture turned into dark brown. The reaction proceeded for 3 min until exhaustion of the acid-base neutralization heat. This spontaneous reaction, with external heating, is a convenient way to fabricate PNCQDs. The bubbles were produced spontaneously to eliminate the excessive heat and served as the templates to confine the dimensions of PNCQDs sample. The structure and the morphology of PNCQDs were characterized by HRTEM. Fig. 1 a showed the characteristic HRTEM image of the as-fabricated PNCQDs. It can be seen that the PNCQDs have an orbicular morphology and homogeneous distribution. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) data was shown in Fig. S3a. The average size of PNCQDs is 3.6 nm, much smaller than early report [29].

To study the chemical composition of the prepared PNCQDs, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed. As shown in Fig. S1a, the XPS spectrum of PNCQDs have five peaks, corresponding to P2p, P2s, C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The P2p peak (Fig. S1b) proves P2p3/2 and P-C binding motifs of PNCQDs [32]. The N1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S1c) demonstrates four peaks, related to C-N-C, N-(C)3, pyridinic N and pyrrolic N, respectively.

As shown in Fig. S2, FTIR was conducted to characterize the functional groups of PNCQDs. A broad absorption peak at 3473 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibrations of COH, revealing that PNCQDs contained plenty of hydroxyl groups [33]. Three sharp peaks at 2858, 1567 and 1408 cm−1 were attributed to the C–H stretching, CN stretching and CH3 umbrella bending, respectively. Other characteristic peaks were ascribed to phosphorus and oxygen-containing groups, such as POH (960 cm−1), POC (1075 cm−1), hydrogen-bonded PO (1148 cm−1), CO vibration (1639 cm−1) and POH bending (2500-2760 cm−1) [33,34]. The zeta potential of PNCQDs (Fig. S4) is -17.82 mV. The abundant functional groups not only provided PNCQDs with negative charges, but also improve the hydrophilicity and stability of PNCQDs in aqueous system.

The optical characteristics of PNCQDs was investigated by fluorescence spectra, the maximum excitation and emission peaks of PNCQDs were found at 403 and 473 nm, respectively. PNCQDs emit bright green radiation under ultraviolet radiation (365 nm) and appear transparent yellow under daylight. Furthermore, the as-prepared PNCQDs exhibited high quantum yield (up to 34.94%).

The aqueous dispersions of AuNRs were characterized by HRTEM, SAED, EDS, DLS, Zeta potential and UV–vis spectroscopy. The EDS results were presented in Fig. S9, showing the elemental composition of AuNRs. HRTEM images were collected to reveal the morphological features of AuNRs. Fig. 1d–f showed the trend of structure change and size distribution of AuNRs and PNCQDs. Representative HRTEM images revealed that the length of initial AuNRs was approximately 83 nm ( Fig. 1b), the corresponding aspect ratio (R) was 2.55 as calculated with the following equation: λmax = 95R + 420 [35] (λmax = 662 nm, λmax refers to the longitudinal absorption wavelength), The SAED (Fig. 1c) pattern displayed distinct ring patterns that agrees well with the standard d-spacing values of Au°, which indicated the presence of Au (200) and Au (422), and demonstrated their crystallinity. HRTEM results were consistent with the results of DLS. The DLS data (Fig. S3b) showed the uniform size distribution of AuNRs (∼29 nm). The fact the sizes is shorter than 83 nm can be explained by equivalent sphere diameter. [36] The length of AuNRs shortened to 8∼16 nm and AuNRs transformed from rod-like structure into nanospheres when H2O2, Fe3+ and HCl were added ( Fig. 1e). As the concentration of Cys increased, the length of AuNRs gradually increased and reached 65 nm in the presence of 25 μM Cys ( Fig. 1f). These morphological changes of AuNRs further proved the sensing mechanism for Cys as described in the following.

The zeta potential of AuNRs was measured as shown in Fig. S5, revealing the exterior of the AuNRs was positively charged. The large zeta potential value also means that AuNRs is stable and can be applied in practical use.

The trend of structure change was also confirmed by the changes of zeta potential (Fig. S6–S8). The zeta potential decreased significantly after PNCQDs/AuNRs was etched from 22.57 mV to 18.65 mV, but when Cys was added, the zeta potential increased to 24.95 mV, demonstrating the sensing mechanism of Cys.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of the sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: To characterize the capability of PNCQDs and AuNRs for sensing of Cys, the absorption spectra were collected. As shown in Fig. 2 a, there are two major absorption peaks of the initial AuNRs located at 528 nm for transverse surface plasmon resonance (TSPR) and 662 nm for LSPR, respectively. The original LSPR absorption peak of AuNRs shifted after etching and only one absorption peak appeared at 528 nm, which can be explained by the following equations.

Equation:
(1) AuNRs + H2O2 + 2H+ → AuNRs´ + 2H2O
(2) Fe3+ +H2O2 → Fe2+ + OOH + H+
(3) Fe2+ +H2O2 → Fe3+ + OH + OH−
(4) AuNRs + OOH + H+ → AuNRs´ +2H2O
(5) AuNRs + OH + H+ → AuNRs´ + 2H2O
(6) Cys + Fe3+ → Fe(Cys)3

Reaction between H2O2 and AuNRs was shown in Eq. (1). When Fe3+ was added in the AuNRs-H2O2-HCl system, Fe3+ reacts with H2O2 to form Fe2+ and OOH, and Fe2+ further react with H2O2. (Eqs. (2), (3)); With strong oxidization capability OOH and OH, AuNRs were oxidized to AuNRs' (the oxidized state of AuNRs) (Eqs. (4), (5)), which resulted in remarkable changes in the shape and LSPR absorption peak of AuNRs. However, After Cys was added to the system, AuNRs showed two absorption peaks. More importantly, with the increase of Cys concentration, the LSPR absorption peak of AuNRs exhibits a gradual red shift accompanied with apparent color changes (Fig. S10). The phenomenon can be explained by the strong coordination between Cys and Fe3+ (Eq. (6)), which effectively inhibited the etching process. As shown in Fig. 2b, the fluorescence intensity of PNCQDs/AuNRs was much lower than that of PNCQDs solution alone. The fluorescence of PNCQDs/AuNRs recovered in the presence of H2O2, but the fluorescence of the system quenched when inhibited by Cys. The reason is that the AuNRs possess greater extinction coefficient than the AuNPs. Particularly important is that AuNRs can quench the fluorescence of PNCQDs effectively due to surface plasmon enhanced energy transfer (SPEET) [37,38]. The LSPR absorption peak exhibits a red shift, (F0 − F)/F0 increases with the increasing of Cys content as well as the peak shift, and (F0-F)/F0 of the system was a function of the Cys content in the range of 0.005–25.0 μM. Based on those results, this non-aggregation colorimetric sensor is suitable for determination of Cys.

Based on these findings, the dual-signal strategy for Cys sensing was established, and the schematic illustration for the designed non-aggregation colorimetric sensor for Cys detection was shown in scheme 1 .

In addition, the fluorescence of PNCQDs and the absorbance of AuNRs are independent. Also, the possibility that HCl, Fe3+, H2O2, Cys of the system directly influence the fluorescence and the absorbance was ruled out (Fig. S11). After adding HCl, Fe3+, H2O2, Cys at high concentrations, no significant difference was observed for the fluorescence and absorbance of the system.

To optimize the system containing 5.0 μM Cys, several factors influencing the peak shift and (F0-F)/F0 of the system were optimized by a univariate approach, including concentration and volume of reagents, pH value, reaction temperature and time.

With lower etching level, the peak shift became more significant. Thus, the peak shift was used for estimating the extent of etching. The fluorescence detraction efficiency is defined as (F0-F)/F0, where F0 and F refer to the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of Cys, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the concentration and volume of reagents
ce:section.ce:para: To study the optimal ratio of AuNRs and PNCQDs, the peak shift and F0/F (Fig. S12) were used as the standard. The F0/F reached the maximum value when the ratio of AuNRs and PNCQDs was 2:3. This means that at this ratio the fluorescent peak will be quenched to the largest extent. However, the peak shift didn't change with the scale. Hence, the optimal proportion is 2:3.

As shown in Fig. S13, the peak shift and (F0-F)/F0 reached maximum values when 0.60 mL AuNRs was used, and the increase of AuNRs resulted in the decrease of the peak shift and (F0-F)/F0. Under the condition of 0.6 mL AuNRs, increasing concentration of H2O2 (Fig. S14) enhanced the peak shift of the system. The peak shift reached the maximum values at 100 μM. The value of (F0-F)/F0 do not change with higher concentration of H2O2 than 100 μM. Thus, 0.60 mL AuNRs solution and 100 μM H2O2 was chosen as the optimal volume.

As shown in Fig. S15, the peak shift of the system increased with the increase of Fe3+ concentration. This is possibly due to that the capability of -SH in Cys coordinating with Fe3+ was larger than the reaction ability of H2O2 and with Fe3+ [39], which leads to enhanced inhibiting ability of Cys with increasing concentration of Fe3+. The peak shift and (F0-F)/F0 reached the maximum value when 16 μM Fe3+ was used. The peak shift and (F0-F)/F0 both decrease with larger amount of Fe3+. This is possibly due to that redundant Fe3+ can etch AuNRs, which caused decrease of the peak shifting. Higher amount of Fe3+ improved the fluorescence (F) of the PNCQDs, leading to reduced (F0-F)/F0. Thus, 16 μM Fe3+ was deemed the optimal concentration.

Under alkaline conditions, Fe3+ would be hydrolyzed into Fe(OH)3. In this study, HCl is used to adjust the pH value of the system. The peak shift of system gradually increased until the concentration of HCl reached 30.0 mM (pH 1.55) when the pH of the solution was 2.0. As shown in Fig. S16, with pH = 2.0, the peak shift of the system reached the maximum. The peak shift decreased when pH value is over 2.0, which is possibly due to that the catalytic reaction was inhibited. Changes of the (F0-F)/F0 in the system can be explained by the inhibition of the catalytic reaction. Thus, the optimal pH value is found to be 2.0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of reaction temperature and time
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of the temperature and time on the peak shift and (F0-F)/F0 of the system were investigated (Figs. S17–S18). Maximum value of peak shift was reached at the reaction temperature of 70 ℃. This is likely due to that the decomposition of Cys at high temperature weakened its inhibition effect. Meanwhile, the reaction time was also evaluated at 5, 10, 15, 20 and 25 min respectively. The results showed that the peak shift of system increased gradually with longer reaction time and reached the maximum at 15 min. The peak shift remained stable when the reaction system was cooled down for another 5 min in ice water. For the fluorescence assay, (F0 − F)/F0 reached the maximum value under the condition of 70 ℃ and 15 min. Higher temperature would decrease (F0 − F)/F0, and longer reaction time did not cause change to (F0 − F)/F0. The changes of fluorescence were also consistent with the aforementioned mechanism. Therefore, 70 ℃ and 15 min were selected as the optimal reaction condition.

As shown in Fig. 3 , under the optimal conditions, the LSPR absorption of AuNRs shifted gradually to longer wavelength with increasing Cys concentrations. The fluorescence of PNCQDs decreased with increasing Cys concentrations. Linear relationships between the peak-shift, (F0-F)/F0 and Cys concentrations were obtained, and both of them were from 0.005 to 25.0 μM. The detection limitation was 0.9 nM (3σ rule) for colorimetry and 1.2 nM (3σ rule) for fluorescence, respectively.

The sensitivity of the present method was compared with other reported studies, as shown in Table 1 . The linear ranges and detection limits of some other reported were presented in Table 1. The present method for Cys has high sensitivity and broad linear range. Additionally, compared with the single signal sensor, the present dual signal sensor of fluorescence and UV–Vis absorption method decreases the interference of background areas, and improvs measurement sensitivity, making the detection results more robust.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and interference of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the sensor to Cys against common interferences was evaluated. Amino acids (Phe, Gly, Pro, Glu, Arg, Lys, Ser, His), GSH, AA, Urea, UA, Glc, K+, Na+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Na2S, Na2SO4 (the exogenous ions and compounds tolerance ratios to Cys are listed in Table. S1) were tested under the same conditions, as demonstrated in Fig. 4 . All these potential interfering substances with various concentrations were tested under parallel conditions in the presence of Cys (Table S1). Even though the concentrations of compounds were much higher than that of Cys, the present method possesses remarkable selectivity to Cys against the interferences of to these compounds. However, the selectivity of the probe was affected by materials containing mercaptoacetic acid and mercaptocypropionic acid. For blood samples, there is not a problem, because blood samples do not contain mercaptoacetic acid and mercaptocypropionic acid. Therefore, the present method is robust for selective determination of Cys in blood samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cys in human urine
ce:section.ce:para: To test the practicality of this method, the standard addition method was used for determination of Cys in human urine samples. These samples were used immediately after processing. All urine samples were diluted 100-fold before use, and the goal was three-fold: to ensure that there was no significant disruption for the determination of the urine, to ensure the concentration of the Cys is in the linear range and to obtain a reasonable recovery of the spiked Cys. As shown in Table S2, the recoveries of the assay were from 96.0% to 104.2% in human urine, for which HPLC analysis was carried out as the standard. The detection results of proposed method were in good accordance with those of HPLC, indicating that this method delivered highly accurate results. The complicated components of some real samples, such as materials of deep coloration, may affect our observation of colorimetric signal by bare eyes, so this probe may not be applied to Cys detection in some complex samples.

In conclusion, a sensor was designed based on the etching effect of OH· produced from Fenton reaction of H2O2 on AuNRs and the specificity of coordination of Cys and Fe3+. A low detection limit (0.9 nM and 1.2 nM) and good linear relationship (0.005–25 μM) were obtained for Cys. Moreover, the sensor exhibited high sensitively, long-term stability and good selectivity. The dual signal sensor was successfully applied for the determination of Cys in urine samples. This dual signal (fluorometric and colorimetric) analysis method demonstrated high potential for application in rapid, sensitive and selective detection of Cys.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by a grant from the Two-Way Support Programs of Sichuan Agricultural University (Project No. 03570113), the Education Department of Sichuan Provincial, PR China (Grant No. 16ZA0039).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.07.084.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cystathionine γ-lyase deficiency mediates neurodegeneration in Huntington’s disease
Cystamine increases l-cysteine levels in Huntington’s disease transgenic mouse brain and in a PC12 model of polyglutamine aggregation
Quantitative reactivity profiling predicts functional cysteines in proteomes
Direct detection of cysteine using functionalized BaTiO3 nanoparticles film based self-powered biosensor
A fluorescence enhancement probe based on BODIPY for the discrimination of cysteine from homocysteine and glutathione
A specific nanoprobe for cysteine based on nitrogen-rich fluorescent quantum dots combined with Cu2+
A facile synthesis of superparamagnetic Fe3O4 nanofibers with superior peroxidase-like catalytic activity for sensitive colorimetric detection of l-cysteine
Electrochemiluminescence performance of nitroolefin-based fluorescein in different solutions and its application for the detection of cysteine
Rapid and accurate HPLC assay for plasma total homocysteine and cysteine in a clinical laboratory setting
Ultrasensitive colorimetric detection of Cu2+ ion based on catalytic oxidation of L-cysteine
Label-free gold nanoparticle-based sensing of cysteine: New peculiarities and prospects
Ultra-sensitive detection of cysteine by gold nanorod assembly
Highly selective and sensitive detection of cysteine with a graphene quantum dots-gold nanoparticles based core-shell nanosensor
A screen-printed carbon electrode modified with a bismuth film and gold nanoparticles for simultaneous stripping voltammetric determination of Zn(II), Pb(II) and Cu(II)
One pot synthesis of dandelion-like polyaniline coated gold nanoparticles composites for electrochemical sensing applications
Colorimetric and fluorometric detection of protamine by using a dual-mode probe consisting of carbon quantum dots and gold nanoparticles
A colorimetric and fluorometric dual-signal sensor for arginine detection by inhibiting the growth of gold nanoparticles/carbon quantum dots composite
A highly sensitive dual-readout assay based on gold nanoclusters for folic acid detection
A highly sensitive and selective cyanide detection using a gold nanoparticle-based dual fluorescence–colorimetric sensor with a wide concentration range
Some interesting properties of metals confined in time and nanometer space of different shapes
Direct High-yield synthesis of high aspect ratio gold nanorods
A fluorescence probe for highly selective and sensitive detection of gaseous ozone based on excited-state intramolecular proton transfer mechanism
Recent advances in carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and biomedical applications
Fluorometric and colorimetric sensor array for discrimination of glucose using enzymatic-triggered dual-signal system consisting of Au@Ag nanoparticles and carbon nanodots
Redox-controlled fluorescent nanoswitch based on reversible disulfide and its application in butyrylcholinesterase activity assay
A ratiometric fluorescent probe based on the pi-stacked graphene oxide and cyanine dye for sensitive detection of bisulfite
In situ fluorogenic and chromogenic reactions for the sensitive dual-readout assay of tyrosinase activity
An “on-off-on” fluorescent nanoprobe for recognition of chromium(VI) and ascorbic acid based on phosphorus/nitrogen dual-doped carbon quantum dot
Pyridine derivative-induced fluorescence in multifunctional modified carbon dots and their application in thermometers
Preparation and growth mechanism of Gold nanorods (NRs) using seed-mediated growth method
Phosphorus and nitrogen dual-doped hollow carbon dot as a nanocarrier for doxorubicin delivery and biological imaging
Facile synthesis of oxygen and sulfur co-doped graphitic carbon nitride fluorescent quantum dots and their application for mercury(ii) detection and bioimaging
Red-green-blue fluorescent hollow carbon nanoparticles isolated from chromatographic fractions for cellular imaging
Colloidal gold and silver triangular nanoprisms
DLS: dynamic backlight luminance scaling of liquid crystal display
Surface plasmon enhanced energy transfer between gold nanorods and fluorophores: application to endocytosis study and RNA detection
Two-photon excited surface plasmon enhanced energy transfer between DAPI and gold nanoparticles: opportunities in intra-cellular imaging and sensing
Design of highly sensitive non-aggregation colorimetric sensor and its application based on inhibiting vitamin C reducing Hg2+
A versatile method for analysis of saliva, plasma and urine for total thiols using HPLC with UV detection

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yanying Wang received doctor degree in College of Chemistry, Sichuan University in 2011. Now she is an associate professor of College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University. Her current interests are modified electrode and bioelectroanalysis.
Ruoxuan Liang is College student of College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University. Her current interests include the development of biosensors and fluorescence sensors.
Wei Liu is postgraduate student of College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University. His current interests include the development of biosensors and fluorescence sensors.
Qingbiao Zhao is a professor in East China Normal University, he is a researcher in material science, information storage materials and strongly correlated materials.
Xiaoyan Zhu is College student of College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University. Her current research interests are simulated-enzyme activated materials.
Li Yang is College student of College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University. Her current research interests are simulated-enzyme activated materials.
Ping Zou is a professor in College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University. His current research interests include the isolation of phytochemicals and the synthesis of natural products.
Xianxiang Wang is a professor in College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University. His current research interests are modified electrode.

Fang Ding is an associate Professor of Suzhou Institute of Systems Medicine, Center for Systems Medicine, Institute of Basic Medical Sciences, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences & Peking Union Medical College. Her research focuses on bioanalytical applications of nanomaterials.

Hanbing Rao received doctor degree in College of Chemistry, Sichuan University in 2010. Now he is an associate Professor of College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University. His current research interests are the preparation and the bioanalytical applications of nanomaterials.
